nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and still freshwater habitats in tropical and subtropical Asia; the 'Taiwan' cultivar is traced to southern Taiwan and is found in shallow, sun-exposed slow-flow waters.

avoidWith: vegetarian plecos that shred soft leaves

summary: A rosette aquarium plant from tropical Asia with lily-pad-style heart-shaped leaves on stems. The 'Taiwan' cultivar tends to stay more submerged than other Nymphoides, producing many underwater leaves before any surface canopy.
careNotes: Nymphoides hydrophylla 'Taiwan' is the dominant trade form of the species and is best known for producing many bright, translucent light-green submerged leaves on short stems rather than a heavy floating canopy — this makes it noticeably more aquarium-friendly than the typical Nymphoides habit. Sister to the North American Banana Plant (Nymphoides aquatica) but lacks that species' characteristic banana-tuber storage organs. Generally considered beginner-friendly under a wide range of conditions; rosette has been reported at roughly 15–30 cm tall and 10–15 cm wide once established, with individual leaves around 3–6 cm across. Has been reported to tolerate temperatures from ~20–30 °C, with most sources clustering on 22–28 °C as the practical aquarium range; pH 6.0–7.5 is the common recommendation though tolerance extends to ~pH 8; soft to moderately hard water (gH ~3–15, kH ~6–12) is generally fine. Strong root feeder — a nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil) or regular root tabs is recommended; water-column ferts are a useful supplement but not the primary driver. Lighting interacts visibly with leaf morphology: under medium-to-high light the plant stays compact with shorter petioles and smaller (~3–4 cm) leaves; under low light leaves enlarge (up to ~6 cm) and stems stretch upward, often producing floating lily-pad leaves at the surface. CO2 is not required but produces denser, more controlled growth — without it, expect a stretchier plant that reaches the surface more readily. Growth is generally medium-to-fast under good conditions; thinning of older and surface leaves keeps the rosette tidy and prevents shading of nearby plants. Propagation is by adventitious plantlets that form on older leaves or at leaf nodes — trim a mature leaf with a portion of stem, let it form roots and a new plantlet, and replant. Side-shoot/runner propagation also works once a clump is established. Best used as a background-to-midground feature plant; smaller tanks may want only a single specimen to avoid crowding. Source spread on adult height was noted (10–30 cm depending on whether the measurement is rosette-only or includes stem-borne floating leaves); the average submerged-rosette dimension is captured here.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Tropica species page provided origin (southern Taiwan), family/genus identity, rosette habit (15–30+ cm tall, 10–15 cm wide), easy care, fast growth, and a low-light tolerance rating with medium CO2 demand. Tropica's search landing page did not deep-link; the species detail URL was confirmed via search.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Confirmed pH 6.0–7.5, adult height ~10–15 cm (4–6 inches), midground-to-background placement, easy-to-moderate care, CO2 not necessary but beneficial, runner/side-shoot propagation, and strong root-feeder behaviour. Light-intensity vs leaf-size trade-off (low light → stretching, surface leaves; brighter light → compact form) cross-confirmed.

additional.name: Gensou Aquascaping
additional.notes: Provided numeric water-parameter ranges that the primary and Buce Plant pages omitted: temperature 22–30 °C, pH 6.0–7.5, gH 3–15, lighting target 50–80 PAR (compact form needs 100+), CO2 optional at 15–30 ppm, growth medium-to-fast, height 15–30 cm with 3–6 cm leaves. Native range stated as tropical/subtropical Asia.

additional.name: Light Fish
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature ~20–27 °C (68–80 °F), pH 6–8, kH 6–12; described as beginner-friendly and fast-growing; native range stated as southern Taiwan with broader Nymphoides spread across Asia and Africa.

propagation: adventitious plantlets from older leaves (trim leaf with stem, allow to root)
side shoots and runners from established crown
